title: MiFID II Transaction Reporting — End-to-End Latency from Execution to ARM Accept Acknowledgment

compliance.assurance_rationale: MiFID II Art.26 — UC provides continuous Splunk telemetry evidence for this regulatory requirement, uplifted from contributing to partial after review confirmed the SPL captures relevant monitoring signals.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that MiFID II Art.26 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.5.25: MiFID II Transaction Reporting — End-to-End Latency from Execution to ARM Accept Acknowledgment.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.5.25' running on index=trading sourcetype="oms:exec" (exec_id, exec_ts); index=reg sourcetype="arm:ack" (exec_id, ack_ts, status), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

app: OMS + ARM via correlated trace IDs in HEC
spl:
index=trading sourcetype="oms:exec" earliest=-7d
| eval et=strptime(exec_ts,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| join type=left exec_id [
    search index=reg sourcetype="arm:ack" status="ACCEPTED" earliest=-7d
    | eval at=strptime(ack_ts,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
    | stats min(at) as first_ack by exec_id
  ]
| eval latency_ms=(first_ack-et)*1000
| where latency_ms>5000 OR isnull(first_ack)
| stats perc95(latency_ms) as p95_ms, count as breaches
description: Timeliness reduces backlogs that cause late reports. Measuring latency from internal execution capture to ARM acceptance ACK supports operational resilience of the reporting chain beyond static “file sent” checks.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for MiFID II compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes reg, trading and sourcetypes arm:ack, oms:exec. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Clock sync NTP monitoring separately; (2) tune SLA ms by instrument; (3) alert reporting ops; (4) dashboard by venue session; (5) retain for audit trail. Indexes required: `index=reg`, `index=trading`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=arm:ack`, `sourcetype=oms:exec`.
knownFalsePositives: Planned maintenance, backups, or batch jobs can drive metrics outside normal bands — correlate with change management windows.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: OMS + ARM via correlated trace IDs in HEC.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=trading` `sourcetype="oms:exec"` (exec_id, exec_ts); `index=reg` `sourcetype="arm:ack"` (exec_id, ack_ts, status).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Clock sync NTP monitoring separately; (2) tune SLA ms by instrument; (3) alert reporting ops; (4) dashboard by venue session; (5) retain for audit trail.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=trading sourcetype="oms:exec" earliest=-7d
| eval et=strptime(exec_ts,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| join type=left exec_id [
    search index=reg sourcetype="arm:ack" status="ACCEPTED" earliest=-7d
    | eval at=strptime(ack_ts,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
    | stats min(at) as first_ack by exec_id
  ]
| eval latency_ms=(first_ack-et)*1000
| where latency_ms>5000 OR isnull(first_ack)
| stats perc95(latency_ms) as p95_ms, count as breaches
```

Understanding this SPL

**MiFID II Transaction Reporting — End-to-End Latency from Execution to ARM Accept Acknowledgment** — Timeliness reduces backlogs that cause late reports. Measuring latency from internal execution capture to ARM acceptance ACK supports operational resilience of the reporting chain beyond static “file sent” checks.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=trading` `sourcetype="oms:exec"` (exec_id, exec_ts); `index=reg` `sourcetype="arm:ack"` (exec_id, ack_ts, status). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): OMS + ARM via correlated trace IDs in HEC. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: trading; **sourcetype**: oms:exec. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=trading, sourcetype="oms:exec", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **et** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Correlates data from a subsearch to enrich the primary result set with additional context.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **latency_ms** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Filters the current rows with `where latency_ms>5000 OR isnull(first_ack)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` aggregates the pipeline (counts, distinct values, sums, percentiles, etc.) into fewer rows.

Output fields: at, breaches, et, first_ack, latency_ms, p95_ms.

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Timechart (p95_ms), Table (slow exec_id), Single value (breaches).

This matters because it catches problems that could lead to audit findings or compliance gaps.
